facts.label: LA FURIA ESPAÑOLA
facts.story: Spain's early nickname, 'La Furia Española' (the Spanish Fury), didn't come from Spanish fans — it was coined by a Dutch newspaper recalling the 16th-century Sack of Antwerp after Spain's aggressive style at the 1920 Antwerp Olympics won silver. The tag stuck through decades of physical, direct football. Only after the 2008–2012 golden generation's tiki-taka era did Spain earn a gentler nickname, 'La Roja,' marking a total shift from muscle to possession.

facts.label: THREE KITS, ONE TOURNAMENT
facts.story: Ahead of their 2014 opener against the Netherlands, FIFA ruled that Spain's registered red home and black away kits were both too dark to satisfy the tournament's light-versus-dark clash rules. Adidas rushed out an emergency all-white third shirt with red accents just for that match, making Spain the first team ever to wear three different kits across a single World Cup. They lost the game 5–1 anyway, and the white kit never reappeared.

1934.headline: Zamora's Injury, Italy's Escape
1934.story: Isidro Lángara and a brace from Chirri sank Brazil 3–1 in Spain's World Cup debut. A bruising 1–1 draw with host Italy forced a next-day replay, and with legendary goalkeeper Ricardo Zamora ruled out by injury, the eventual champions escaped 1–0, ending Spain's run after just two matches.

annotations.label: BATTLE OF FLORENCE
annotations.story: The scoreless brawl that forced the replay was so vicious that history remembers it as the Battle of Florence — sliding tackles, elbows, and a battered Spanish side that limped into the next day's decider missing not just Zamora but several starters too bruised to walk out again.

annotations.label: THE DIVINE ONE'S JUMPER
annotations.story: While teammates wore plain red, keeper Ricardo Zamora patrolled his box in a cloth cap and thick white polo-neck jumper — a uniform so distinct that fans nicknamed him 'El Divino.' He'd broken his sternum for Spain once already, in a legendary 1929 win over England, and kept playing anyway.

annotations.label: MUSSOLINI'S STAGE
annotations.story: Rome draped its stadiums in fascist salutes and Il Duce's portrait that summer, and whispers followed the hosts everywhere — including allegations that referee Ivan Eklind had been summoned to Mussolini's private box before matches. Nothing was ever proven, but Italy's suspiciously convenient path to the title still hasn't shaken the theory.

1934.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, black socks

1950.headline: Zarra's Header Shocks England
1950.story: Telmo Zarra's header beat England 1–0 in Rio, still considered one of the great World Cup upsets. Spain topped their first group unbeaten, but the final round brought a 6–1 mauling by Brazil at the Maracanã and a loss to Sweden, leaving them fourth — their best finish for six decades.

annotations.label: THE MAN WHO COULDN'T SAY ZARRA
annotations.story: Radio commentator Matías Prats called the winning goal live from the Maracanã, but decades of Spaniards misheard him — a speech impediment made him swap his C's and Z's on air, so millions heard 'gol de Farra' instead of Zarra. The mispronunciation became folklore in its own right.

annotations.label: THE EAGLE ON THE CHEST
annotations.story: The crest stitched above the heart wasn't the crown Spain wears today — it was the Eagle of Saint John, adopted after the Civil War and carrying four medieval coats of arms beneath a halo. The design stayed on Spanish shirts for three more decades, long after the reasons for it were gone.

annotations.label: LOCKED AWAY IN RIO
annotations.story: Coach Benito Díaz ran the squad like a boarding school, dictating meals, tactics, and even phone calls from a hotel beneath Corcovado. The staff eventually switched hotels entirely, worried the young squad was too distracted by the attention they were drawing in Rio's nightlife to focus on Brazil looming in the final round.

1950.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, black socks

1962.headline: Di Stéfano Never Plays
1962.story: A late Adelardo strike wasn't enough as Brazil edged Spain 2–1 in a rain-soaked Chile decider, sending the reigning champions through and Spain out after the group stage. José Villalonga's side, built around a nominally star-studded roster, never got to use Alfredo Di Stéfano, injured and left out of every match.

annotations.label: PEIRÓ'S LAST-GASP WINNER
annotations.story: Joaquín Peiró's goal in the 90th minute against Mexico was Spain's only win of the tournament, but it kept their hopes alive into the final round against Brazil. Without it, the frantic, ultimately unsuccessful chase against the reigning champions in the rain at Viña del Mar would never have mattered at all.

annotations.label: THE NUMBER HE NEVER WORE OUT
annotations.story: Alfredo Di Stéfano couldn't play through his injury, but he still travelled with the squad and kept the No. 6 shirt on his back — his usual No. 9 already claimed by Francisco Gento. It's the closest the greatest player of his generation ever got to appearing in a World Cup.

annotations.label: CHILE'S EARTHQUAKE WORLD CUP
annotations.story: The host nation was still rebuilding when the tournament kicked off — the 1960 earthquake, the strongest ever recorded, had flattened whole towns two years earlier. Chile pressed ahead anyway under the defiant slogan 'Because we have nothing, we will do everything,' raising new stadiums from the rubble for a tournament nobody expected it to be able to stage.

1962.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, black socks

1966.headline: Eliminated Again at Villa Park
1966.story: Pirri's late strike wasn't enough in a 2–1 opening loss to Argentina, and although Amancio and Sanchís rallied past Switzerland, José Fusté's goal against West Germany at Villa Park couldn't stop a 2–1 defeat. Spain went out in the group stage for the second time in three tournaments.

annotations.label: THE TEAM THAT WENT TO WEMBLEY
annotations.story: The side that eliminated them in Birmingham went all the way to Wembley two weeks later, losing the final to the hosts in extra time — small comfort for a Spain team that got no closer to the knockout rounds than a single José Fusté strike against the eventual runners-up.

annotations.label: REAL MADRID IN RED
annotations.story: Half of Spain's attack that summer — Amancio, Sanchís, the Real Madrid contingent — spent every league Saturday in all white. Villa Park was one of the rare stages where they traded the Bernabéu's colors for the deep red of the national shirt, scoring the only two goals that kept Spain's tournament alive past the opener.

annotations.label: A DOG NAMED PICKLES
annotations.story: Weeks before the tournament, the actual World Cup trophy was stolen from a public exhibition in London and later found wrapped in newspaper under a garden hedge — by a mixed-breed dog named Pickles. Spain arrived to play for a trophy that had briefly gone missing entirely, guarded from then on like nuclear codes.

1966.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, black socks

1978.headline: Same Script, Group Stage Again
1978.story: Dani's consolation goal couldn't prevent a 2–1 opening loss to Austria in Buenos Aires, and although Asensi's strike beat Sweden and Spain held Brazil scoreless, the group's math left them short. It was the same story as 1966 and 1962 — group stage, then home.

annotations.label: SPAIN'S FIRST BRAZIL SHUTOUT
annotations.story: Nobody remembers a scoreless draw, but this one mattered: it was the first time in four World Cup meetings that Spain had kept Brazil off the scoresheet entirely, after conceding nine goals combined in 1934 and 1950. It wasn't enough to advance, but it proved the age of Brazilian routs was over.

annotations.label: THE EAGLE'S WINGS OPEN
annotations.story: A year earlier, in 1977, the federation quietly redesigned the crest on Spain's shirt — the Eagle of Saint John's wings drawn wider and more triumphant, even as Francisco Franco had been dead for three years and the eagle itself, a wartime relic, had only a few seasons left before it disappeared for good.

annotations.label: FOOTBALL NEXT DOOR TO TERROR
annotations.story: General Jorge Videla's junta staged the tournament as a public-relations triumph while, seven hundred metres from the Buenos Aires stadium hosting the final, the Navy Mechanics School operated as one of the regime's deadliest torture centers. Players competed inside the same propaganda machine the Mothers of the Plaza de Mayo were already marching against.

1978.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, black socks

1982.headline: Hosts Fall Short at Home
1982.story: Hosting their first World Cup since Franco's death, Spain stumbled early as Northern Ireland beat them 1–0 despite playing a man down. Jesús María Zamora's goal wasn't enough in a 2–1 second-round loss to West Germany, and a goalless stalemate with England sent the hosts out before the semi-finals.

annotations.label: TEN MEN, ONE GOAL, ENOUGH
annotations.story: Mal Donaghy was sent off in the 62nd minute for a foul the referee didn't even see himself — he trusted his linesman — and Northern Ireland still nursed a 1–0 lead to the final whistle. Playing the last half-hour a man light against the hosts remains one of the great backs-to-the-wall World Cup jobs.

annotations.label: AN ORANGE IN A SPAIN SHIRT
annotations.story: The tournament mascot, Naranjito, was a grinning cartoon orange dressed in the exact red shirt and blue shorts the players wore that summer — sportswear as advertising, designed to make the host's colors feel modern and cheerful barely a year after a coup attempt had nearly derailed the whole country.

annotations.label: SIXTEEN MONTHS AFTER THE COUP
annotations.story: On 23 February 1981, Civil Guard officers stormed the Spanish parliament with rifles raised, an attempted coup that came within hours of dragging the country back toward military rule. Sixteen months later, the World Cup arrived instead — a tournament staged, deliberately, as proof the young democracy could hold.

1982.homeColors: red shirt with white trim, blue shorts, black socks (Adidas debut kit)

1986.headline: Butragueño's Four-Goal Night
1986.story: Emilio 'El Buitre' Butragueño scored four goals in a stunning 5–1 demolition of Denmark in the Round of 16, one of the tournament's signature performances. Belgium eliminated Spain on penalties in the quarter-final after Antonio Señor's equalizer forced extra time, the shootout ending 5–4 to the Belgians.

annotations.label: THE MISS THAT SENT BELGIUM THROUGH
annotations.story: Antonio Señor's 85th-minute equalizer forced extra time, but in the shootout it was 19-year-old substitute Eloy Olaya, the youngest man on the pitch, who saw his low effort saved by Jean-Marie Pfaff. Belgium converted their next kick and eliminated the team that had just put five goals past Denmark two rounds earlier.

annotations.label: SPAIN SWITCHES SUPPLIERS
annotations.story: For the first time since 1981, Spain took the field without Adidas's three stripes — French brand Le Coq Sportif had taken over the kit contract in 1984, dressing Butragueño's team in a red shirt with the brand's rooster crest woven quietly into the collar, a detail most fans never even noticed.

annotations.label: THE VULTURE AND HIS QUINTA
annotations.story: Butragueño's four goals against Denmark came from a nickname earned at Real Madrid — 'El Buitre,' the Vulture, for his predatory finishing inside the box. He was the most famous face of La Quinta del Buitre, five academy products who'd just taken over Real Madrid's midfield and attack, several of whom lined up alongside him in Mexico that summer.

1986.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, black socks (Le Coq Sportif)

1990.headline: Míchel's Hat-Trick, Then Yugoslavia
1990.story: Míchel's hat-trick demolished South Korea 3–1 to help Spain top the group, but Dragan Stojković tormented their defense in the Round of 16. Julio Salinas pulled one back, yet Spain fell 2–1 to Yugoslavia in Verona, one win short of matching the previous tournament's quarter-final run.

annotations.label: STOJKOVIĆ'S SOLO SEMI-FINAL AUDITION
annotations.story: Dragan Stojković scored twice to send Yugoslavia through, including a mazy solo run through half of Spain's defense in extra time that's still replayed as one of the great individual World Cup goals. Julio Salinas' equalizer had bought Spain thirty more minutes; Stojković needed only a few of them to end their tournament.

annotations.label: A SOFTER COLLAR, A LOUDER NUMBER
annotations.story: Le Coq Sportif refreshed the kit for Italia '90 with a yellow-trimmed V-neck that gave Spain's shirt a rounder, more casual collar than anything Adidas had produced. On the back, Butragueño's flocked No. 9 — thick, chunky lettering typical of the era — became one of the most recognizable numbers of the tournament.

annotations.label: THE CUP THAT REWROTE THE OFFSIDE LAW
annotations.story: Italia '90 finished as the lowest-scoring World Cup ever played, so defensive and cynical that FIFA rewrote the offside law and introduced the back-pass rule almost immediately afterward. Spain's six goals in four matches, built around one player's hat-trick, actually ranked among the tournament's livelier team performances by comparison.

1990.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, black socks (Le Coq Sportif)

1994.headline: The Elbow Referee Never Saw
1994.story: Luis Enrique needed his nose packed with cotton after Mauro Tassotti's elbow drew blood in the box, but referee Sándor Puhl waved play on and Roberto Baggio's late winner sent Italy through 2–1. It remains one of the World Cup's most notorious missed penalty calls, denying Spain a semi-final chance.

annotations.label: THE REF WHO MISSED IT, PROMOTED
annotations.story: Hungarian referee Sándor Puhl waved away the clearest penalty of the tournament, and FIFA's response was to hand him the 1998 final four years later. Luis Enrique's blood-soaked shirt became the enduring image of the injustice; the video review that might have caught it was still a decade away.

annotations.label: THE 'EQUIPMENT' ERA SHIRT
annotations.story: Adidas dressed Spain in its 'Equipment' line that summer — a slim, linear-branded tricot shirt with a plain ribbed collar, part of the same design family the brand used across a dozen federations. Even the socks couldn't settle on one identity, drifting between black and blue across different kit runs of the same tournament.

annotations.label: PEP'S ONE WORLD CUP GOAL
annotations.story: Buried in the group-stage rout of Bolivia is a footnote: Josep Guardiola's only World Cup goal, scored as a 23-year-old holding midfielder years before he'd redefine how Spain played the game entirely. Nobody watching that day could have guessed they'd just watched the future architect of tiki-taka score for his country.

1994.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, socks shifting from black to blue (Adidas)

1998.headline: Nigeria's Opening Day Shock
1998.story: Nigeria stunned Spain 3–2 in the opener despite goals from Fernando Hierro and a teenage Raúl. José Luis Chilavert frustrated them to a 0–0 draw with Paraguay, and although Spain routed Bulgaria 6–1 in the finale, Nigeria's group-stage upset had already ended the tournament before it truly began.

annotations.label: TWO GOALS, ALWAYS CHASING
annotations.story: Hierro pulled one back in the 21st minute and Raúl added a second just after the restart, but Nigeria's night was already won — the decisive third goal arrived before either build could matter, sealing the opening upset that undid Spain's entire campaign before it started.

annotations.label: NAVY PANELS, YELLOW STITCHING
annotations.story: Adidas broke from Spain's usual solid red with bold navy panels running down each side of the 1998 shirt, picked out in yellow stitching that echoed the flag more than any kit in a decade. It was a sharper, more angular look for a squad that, on the pitch, still played with 1980s directness.

annotations.label: HIERRO'S THIRD SHOT
annotations.story: Fernando Hierro had already played in 1990 and 1994 by the time Nigeria caught Spain cold in Nantes — a full World Cup cycle of near-misses stacking up behind him. He'd finish his career with four World Cups and never get past a quarter-final, the defining frustration of Spain's pre-golden-generation era.

1998.homeColors: red shirt, navy shorts, blue socks (Adidas)

matches.scorers: Hierro 6', Enrique 18', Morientes 55', Morientes 81', Bachev OG 88', Kiko 90'+4'

2002.headline: Two Goals Wiped Off in Korea
2002.story: Iker Casillas saved two penalties to beat the Republic of Ireland in the Round of 16, but the quarter-final against co-host South Korea turned sour. The referee disallowed two Spanish goals, and Joaquín's missed spot-kick sent South Korea through 5–3 on penalties in one of the tournament's most contested matches.

annotations.label: TWO GOALS, ZERO ON THE BOARD
annotations.story: Rubén Baraja's strike was ruled out for a foul only the referee seemed to see, and Fernando Morientes' follow-up was flagged offside on a call replays never supported — television showed the ball still in play when the cross went in. Spain lost the shootout still convinced both disallowed goals should have ended the match in regulation.

annotations.label: THE CROWN, RESTORED
annotations.story: By 2002 the badge over the heart bore the same royal crown it wears today — the last vestiges of the Francoist Eagle of Saint John had been retired years earlier as Spain's restored monarchy reclaimed its old symbols. Three generations of players had worn someone else's war on their chest without ever choosing to.

annotations.label: A DROPPED BOTTLE OF COLOGNE
annotations.story: First-choice keeper Santiago Cañizares was ruled out days before the tournament after dropping a glass aftershave bottle on his own foot and severing a tendon — a freak accident that handed the gloves to a 21-year-old reserve named Iker Casillas, who saved two penalties against Ireland and never gave the shirt back for a decade.

2002.homeColors: red shirt, navy shorts, blue socks (Adidas)

2006.headline: Zidane Ends the Run
2006.story: David Villa scored a brace in a 4–0 opening rout of Ukraine and finished the group stage with three goals. Zinedine Zidane, playing what he had announced as his final tournament, tore through Spain's defense in the Round of 16 as France won 3–1, ending Spain's run in Hanover.

annotations.label: ZIDANE'S STOPPAGE-TIME DAGGER
annotations.story: Franck Ribéry's opener and Patrick Vieira's penalty had already turned the tie France's way when Zinedine Zidane, in the tournament he'd announced would be his last, chipped in a stoppage-time third to finish it off — the kind of goal that made the eventual runner-up feel less like an aging team and more like a farewell tour.

annotations.label: PINSTRIPES AND CURVES
annotations.story: Adidas dressed Spain in its 'Aquila' template for Germany 2006 — thin yellow pinstripes running through the red, curved side panels breaking from the usual straight cut, and the brand's three stripes swept onto the shoulders instead of the sleeves. It was a busier, more tailored look than any Spain shirt before it.

annotations.label: THE BLUEPRINT FOR 2008
annotations.story: Luis Aragonés had only just taken over and was still experimenting with the short-passing, possession-heavy approach the press mocked as too soft for a team raised on 'La Furia.' Two years later that same approach won Euro 2008; in 2006 it was still just an idea getting battle-tested against France's aging superstars.

2006.homeColors: red shirt, navy shorts, black socks (Adidas)

2010.headline: Iniesta's 116th-Minute Winner
2010.story: A shock 0–1 opening loss to Switzerland nearly derailed everything, but Spain won every remaining match 1–0, including Carles Puyol's headed semi-final winner over Germany. Andrés Iniesta settled the final in the 116th minute against the Netherlands, and Spain became the first European team to win a World Cup outside Europe.

annotations.label: THE MESSAGE UNDER THE SHIRT
annotations.story: When Iniesta ripped off his shirt after the winning goal, the undershirt beneath read 'Dani Jarque, siempre con nosotros' — a tribute to his close friend and former youth teammate, an Espanyol captain who'd died of a heart attack the year before. The image became more famous in Spain than the goal itself.

annotations.label: THE STAR THAT CAME NEXT
annotations.story: The gold star that's sewn above Spain's crest on every replica shirt sold since didn't exist yet in Johannesburg — it was earned that July night and stitched onto the kit for the very first time the following season, the permanent, wearable proof of a first world title.

annotations.label: CELEBRATING THROUGH A RECESSION
annotations.story: The economy was contracting, unemployment had passed 20 percent, and the government was about to impose brutal austerity cuts when the final whistle blew in Johannesburg. Millions still flooded the streets waving flags anyway — captain Iker Casillas said afterward that for one night, the celebration made everyone forget the crisis entirely.

2010.homeColors: red shirt with gold trim, navy shorts, black socks (Adidas)
2010.awayColors: navy shirt with red-and-yellow flag-striped trim on the collar and shoulders, navy shorts (Adidas) — worn in the final

imageCredit.game: Spain vs Germany, 2010 Semi-final — CC BY-SA 3.0 / Wikimedia Commons

2014.headline: The Emergency White Kit Disaster
2014.story: Defending champions Spain were forced into an emergency all-white third kit for the opener because FIFA ruled their red home and black away were both too dark against Netherlands orange — then lost the match 5–1 anyway, with Robin van Persie's diving header and two Arjen Robben goals dismantling them. A group-stage exit followed within a week.

annotations.label: THE PASS THAT ENDED AN ERA
annotations.story: Iker Casillas, the hero of 2010, played a blind pass straight into Arjen Robben's path for the Netherlands' fourth goal — the single image that came to define the collapse of Spain's golden generation. Within a week the whole squad, defending champions less than four years removed from Johannesburg, was on a plane home.

annotations.label: THE FIRST SHIRT WITH THE STAR
annotations.story: This was the first Spain kit to actually carry that gold 2010 champions badge on the chest — a two-tone red pinstripe design so light Adidas called it the thinnest World Cup shirt it had ever produced. The gold trim was meant to celebrate a title; instead it became a costume for the fastest fall from grace in the competition's history.

annotations.label: THE GENERATION THAT RAN OUT OF ROAD
annotations.story: Most of the starting eleven that lost to the Netherlands had also started the 2010 final four years earlier — the same core, without a real off-season, cycling through three straight major tournaments together. Age and fatigue finally caught up with the golden generation in Brazil, and Vicente del Bosque never fielded that same spine again.

2014.homeColors: red shirt, navy shorts, black socks (Adidas)
2014.awayColors: black shirt with red trim (Adidas); emergency all-white third kit with red accents worn vs Netherlands after FIFA ruled the registered kits too dark

2018.headline: Fired Coach, Then Penalties
2018.story: Julen Lopetegui was fired as coach two days before the opener after secretly signing with Real Madrid, and Diego Costa's brace salvaged a 3–3 draw with a Cristiano Ronaldo-inspired Portugal. Under interim boss Fernando Hierro, Spain lost on penalties to host Russia in the Round of 16, ending Andrés Iniesta's final World Cup.

annotations.label: RONALDO'S HAT-TRICK, LAST KICK
annotations.story: Diego Costa's second goal put Spain 3-2 up with the clock running out, and it looked like a statement debut for the interim regime — until Cristiano Ronaldo curled a free kick over the wall in the final minute to complete his own hat-trick and steal a point that felt, in the chaos of that week, like a defeat.

annotations.label: A LOVE LETTER TO '94
annotations.story: The home shirt's diamond-shaped flash of yellow, blue, and red down the right side wasn't new — it was a direct homage to the shirt Spain wore at USA '94, right down to moving the Adidas logo back to the chest's center, exactly where it sat twenty-four years earlier. Adidas insisted it was pure nostalgia.

annotations.label: FORTY-EIGHT HOURS' NOTICE
annotations.story: Fernando Hierro had never managed a senior team above Spain's third tier when RFEF president Luis Rubiales handed him the job — with the opening match forty-eight hours away and a locker room still reeling from watching its actual coach get fired live on Spanish television for signing with Real Madrid.

2018.homeColors: red shirt, navy shorts, black socks (Adidas)
2018.awayColors: pale 'Halo Blue' shirt with red trim and an 1980s-inspired geometric pattern (Adidas)

2022.headline: Morocco's Shootout Ambush
2022.story: Eighteen-year-old Gavi became one of the youngest World Cup goalscorers in a 7–0 opening rout of Costa Rica. Spain then collapsed late to lose 2–1 to Japan in a controversial reversal, and Morocco eliminated them in the Round of 16, saving three penalties in a shootout that ended Luis Enrique's tenure.
2022.homeColors: red shirt with white and gold geometric pattern, navy shorts (Adidas)
2022.awayColors: sky-blue shirt with an art-deco pattern inspired by Spain's 1982 World Cup logo, royal blue trim (Adidas)
